SaaS: Software as a Service. Renting access to software hosted by the vendor, paid via recurring subscription rather than a perpetual licence.
IaaS: Infrastructure as a Service. Virtual machines, storage, and networking rented from a cloud provider instead of running your own hardware.
PaaS: Platform as a Service. A managed environment where developers deploy code without managing servers, containers, or operating systems.
ORM: Object-Relational Mapping. A layer that translates between programming-language objects and relational database rows, abstracting SQL behind method calls.
MVC: Model-View-Controller. An architectural pattern that separates data (Model), presentation (View), and input handling (Controller).
SPA: Single-Page Application. A web app that loads one HTML document and rewrites it dynamically via JavaScript, avoiding full page reloads.
SSR: Server-Side Rendering. Generating the full HTML of a page on the server before sending it to the browser, as opposed to client-side rendering.
CSR: Client-Side Rendering. Building the page DOM entirely in the browser via JavaScript after an initial empty HTML shell is delivered.
REST: Representational State Transfer. An architectural style for APIs using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on resource URLs.
JSON-RPC: JSON Remote Procedure Call. A lightweight protocol that encodes function calls and responses as JSON objects, transported over HTTP or WebSockets.
CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment. Automated pipelines that build, test, and deploy code on every commit.
OKLCH: A perceptually uniform colour space using Lightness, Chroma, and Hue. Produces consistent perceived brightness across all hues, unlike HSL.
OOCSS: Object-Oriented CSS. A methodology that separates structure from skin and container from content, promoting reusable CSS classes.
CSS Layers: The @layer rule in CSS, allowing authors to define explicit cascade priority between groups of styles, eliminating specificity wars.
Specificity: The algorithm browsers use to determine which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element. Based on selector weight: ID > class > element.
Fetch API: The modern browser interface for making HTTP requests, replacing XMLHttpRequest. Returns Promises and supports streaming.
ES Modules: The native JavaScript module system using import/export syntax, loaded by browsers without a bundler.
WebSocket: A persistent, full-duplex communication channel over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time data exchange between browser and server.
Docker: A container runtime that packages applications with their dependencies into isolated filesystem images, sharing the host kernel.
Kubernetes: An orchestration platform for managing containerised workloads across clusters. Handles scheduling, scaling, and self-healing.
FreeBSD: An open-source Unix operating system descended from the Berkeley Software Distribution. Known for ZFS, Jails, and its licence model.
ZFS: Zettabyte File System. A combined filesystem and volume manager with checksumming, snapshots, clones, and built-in RAID.
Jails: FreeBSD's OS-level virtualisation. Lightweight isolation of processes, filesystems, and network stacks without a hypervisor.
WireGuard: A modern VPN protocol using state-of-the-art cryptography. One C file, ~4,000 lines, replaces IPsec and OpenVPN.
Caddy: A web server with automatic HTTPS via built-in ACME client. Written in Go. Often used as a reverse proxy.
Rust: A systems programming language emphasising memory safety without garbage collection, using ownership and borrowing rules enforced at compile time.
Lua: A lightweight embeddable scripting language. 30 KB runtime, first-class functions, metatables for OOP. Used in game engines and web servers.
SQLite: A self-contained SQL database engine stored in a single file. No server process. Handles terabytes with zero configuration.
PostgreSQL: An advanced open-source relational database with MVCC, JSON support, full-text search, and extensibility via custom types and functions.
nginx: A high-performance HTTP server and reverse proxy using an event-driven, non-blocking architecture. Serves static files and proxies upstream applications.
Webpack: A JavaScript module bundler that resolves dependency graphs and outputs optimised bundles. Largely replaced by Vite and native ES Modules.
npm: Node Package Manager. The default registry and CLI for JavaScript packages. Manages dependency trees in node_modules.
Scrum: An agile framework using fixed-length Sprints, daily stand-ups, and defined roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team).
Event Sourcing: A pattern that stores every state change as an immutable event rather than overwriting current state. The event log becomes the source of truth.
CQRS: Command Query Responsibility Segregation. Separates read models from write models, allowing independent optimisation of each path.
Microservices: An architecture that decomposes an application into small, independently deployable services communicating over network protocols.
Monolith: A single deployable unit containing all application logic. Often contrasted with microservices but not inherently inferior.
TypeScript: A typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JS. Adds static type checking at build time.
Sass: A CSS preprocessor adding variables, nesting, and mixins. Largely obsoleted by native CSS custom properties, nesting, and @layer.
CSS-in-JS: A pattern where CSS is authored inside JavaScript files, generating styles at runtime. Adds bundle weight and runtime overhead.
Serverless: A cloud execution model where the provider allocates resources per request. You write functions, the vendor manages infrastructure.
Unix Philosophy: Design principles favouring small, composable programs that do one thing well, connected via text streams.
Demoscene: A computer art subculture producing real-time audiovisual presentations (demos) within extreme size constraints, often 64 KB or less.
CAST: The templating language used by CASTD. Five delimiter pairs for variables, conditions, slots, comments, and expressions.
light-dark(): A CSS function that returns one of two values depending on the computed color-scheme. Eliminates media-query duplication for dark mode.
Capsicum: A capability-based security framework in FreeBSD. One syscall (cap_enter) permanently removes all access to global namespaces. The process keeps only its existing file descriptors, each restricted via cap_rights_limit(). Irreversible. Compiled into FreeBSD 10.0 by default since 2014.
pf: Packet Filter. The firewall in FreeBSD (and OpenBSD). Stateful, with anchors, tables, and a concise rule syntax.
nftables: The Linux packet classification framework replacing iptables. Uses a unified syntax for filtering, NAT, and mangling.
Redis: An in-memory data structure store used as database, cache, and message broker. Single-threaded, sub-millisecond latency.
SSH: Secure Shell. A cryptographic protocol for secure remote login, command execution, and tunnelling over untrusted networks.
TLS: Transport Layer Security. The cryptographic protocol securing HTTPS, email, and other network traffic. Successor to SSL.
HTTP/2: The second major version of HTTP. Binary framing, multiplexed streams over a single TCP connection, header compression.
HTTP/3: HTTP over QUIC. Replaces TCP with UDP-based transport, eliminating head-of-line blocking. Built-in encryption.
ACME: Automatic Certificate Management Environment. The protocol Let's Encrypt uses to issue and renew TLS certificates without manual intervention.
IPsec: Internet Protocol Security. A suite of protocols for authenticating and encrypting IP packets. Complex configuration, largely replaced by WireGuard for VPNs.
OpenVPN: An open-source VPN solution using SSL/TLS for key exchange. Flexible but verbose configuration compared to WireGuard.
AWS: Amazon Web Services. The dominant cloud computing platform offering IaaS, PaaS, and managed services.
systemd: The init system and service manager in most Linux distributions. Manages processes, logging, networking, and device events. Controversial for its scope.
cron: The Unix job scheduler. Executes commands at specified times using a five-field time expression (minute, hour, day, month, weekday).
rsync: A fast, incremental file transfer utility. Transfers only the differences between source and destination using a rolling checksum algorithm.
tmux: A terminal multiplexer. Runs multiple shell sessions in one terminal, supports detaching and reattaching, split panes, and session persistence.
POSIX: Portable Operating System Interface. A family of IEEE standards defining Unix-compatible APIs, shell behaviour, and utility conventions.
ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. The four guarantees a database transaction must provide for reliability.
MVCC: Multi-Version Concurrency Control. A database technique where readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers. Used in PostgreSQL.
GraphQL: A query language for APIs where the client specifies exactly which fields it needs. Developed by Facebook, alternative to REST.
gRPC: A high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers and HTTP/2. Supports streaming, code generation, and strong typing.
Vite: A frontend build tool using native ES Modules for instant dev server startup and Rollup for production builds. Replaces Webpack.
Bun: An all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, transpiler, and package manager. Written in Zig, designed for speed.
Deno: A secure JavaScript/TypeScript runtime by the creator of Node.js. Built-in TypeScript support, URL imports, and sandboxed permissions.
CNCF: Cloud Native Computing Foundation. The Linux Foundation project hosting Kubernetes, Prometheus, Envoy, and other cloud-native infrastructure projects.
PyCon: The annual Python community conference. Held in multiple countries, featuring talks, sprints, and community gatherings.
KubeCon: The Cloud Native Computing Foundation's flagship conference for Kubernetes and cloud-native technologies.
FOSDEM: Free and Open Source Developers' European Meeting. Annual two-day conference in Brussels, free to attend, no registration.
GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation. EU law governing the collection, processing, and storage of personal data. Effective since 2018.
WebAssembly: A binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine. Runs near-native speed in browsers alongside JavaScript.
HAProxy: A high-performance TCP/HTTP load balancer and reverse proxy. Event-driven, widely used for traffic distribution.
Terraform: An infrastructure-as-code tool by HashiCorp. Declares cloud resources in HCL files and manages their lifecycle.
Ansible: An agentless automation tool for configuration management, application deployment, and orchestration. Uses YAML playbooks over SSH.
Prometheus: A time-series monitoring system with a pull-based model, PromQL query language, and built-in alerting.
Grafana: A visualisation platform for metrics, logs, and traces. Connects to Prometheus, Loki, InfluxDB, and dozens of other data sources.
Linus Torvalds: Creator and principal developer of the Linux kernel. Also wrote Git. Maintains kernel development from Portland, Oregon.
Martin Fowler: Software author and Chief Scientist at ThoughtWorks. Coined 'Microservices' with James Lewis. Wrote Refactoring, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture, and the 'Monolith First' essay.
Rich Harris: Creator of Svelte and Rollup. Former graphics editor at The New York Times. Demonstrated that the Virtual DOM is 'pure overhead'.
David Heinemeier Hansson: Creator of Ruby on Rails. Co-founder of Basecamp and HEY. Now advocates software ownership over SaaS subscriptions with ONCE.
Greg Kroah-Hartman: Senior Linux kernel maintainer responsible for stable releases. In 2024, removed Russian-affiliated maintainers from the MAINTAINERS file on compliance grounds.
Greg Young: Popularised Event Sourcing and CQRS as architectural patterns. Author of Versioning in an Event Sourced System.
Ken Schwaber: Co-creator of Scrum with Jeff Sutherland. Founded the Scrum Alliance, later Scrum.org. Admitted that 75 per cent of organisations using Scrum will not succeed.
Ron Jeffries: Co-creator of Extreme Programming. Agile Manifesto signatory. In 2018, wrote 'Developers Should Abandon Agile' — a rare recantation from an original author.
Dave Thomas: Agile Manifesto signatory. Co-author of The Pragmatic Programmer. Declared 'Agile is Dead' in 2014, arguing the word had been hijacked by consultancies.
James Lewis: Software architect at ThoughtWorks. Co-coined the term 'Microservices' with Martin Fowler in their 2014 article defining the architectural style.
Rich Felker: Author of musl libc, a clean-room lightweight C standard library for Linux. Vocal advocate for POSIX correctness and minimal PID 1 design.
Lennart Poettering: Creator of systemd and PulseAudio. His replacement of SysVinit with a monolithic init system remains one of the most divisive decisions in Linux history.
Copy-on-Write: A storage strategy where data is never overwritten in place. New data is written to a new location, then the pointer is updated. Guarantees consistency after power failure.
Merkle tree: A hash tree where every leaf node is labelled with the hash of a data block and every non-leaf node is labelled with the hash of its children. Used by ZFS for end-to-end checksumming.
DKMS: Dynamic Kernel Module Support. A framework for building kernel modules that automatically recompile when the kernel is updated. Used by ZFS on Linux.
CDDL: Common Development and Distribution Licence. Sun Microsystems' open-source licence, intentionally incompatible with the GPL. The licence under which ZFS was released.
Jeff Bonwick: Engineer at Sun Microsystems who designed the Slab Allocator and co-created ZFS with Matthew Ahrens. Built the filesystem that trusts mathematics over hardware.
Salvatore Sanfilippo: Sicilian developer known as antirez. Created Redis in 2009, a single-threaded in-memory data structure server. Maintained it personally for fifteen years.
Theo de Raadt: Founder and leader of the OpenBSD project. Forked OpenSSH in 1999. Three decades of security-first development, auditing every line, removing attack surface instead of adding features.
Privilege Separation: A security architecture that splits a process into a small privileged monitor and a larger unprivileged worker. Limits damage when the worker is compromised. Pioneered by OpenSSH in 2002.
OpenBSD: A security-focused open-source Unix operating system. Produced OpenSSH, LibreSSL, OpenBGPD, and the pledge()/unveil() system calls. Known for code audits and minimal defaults.
Andrew Tridgell: Australian computer scientist. Created rsync (1996), Samba (Windows file sharing on Unix), ccache, and co-developed Git's delta compression with Linus Torvalds.
Rolling Checksum: A hash that can be updated incrementally as a window slides across data. Enables rsync to find matching blocks in O(n) instead of O(n squared). Based on Adler-32.
FFmpeg: A universal multimedia framework handling over 100 audio and video codecs. 1.5 million lines of C. Powers YouTube, Netflix, VLC, Spotify, Chrome. Created by Fabrice Bellard in 2000.
Fabrice Bellard: French computer scientist. Created FFmpeg, QEMU, TinyCC, JSLinux, and Bellard's formula. Computed 2.7 trillion digits of pi on a desktop PC. Builds fundamental tools, then moves on.
VNET: Virtual Network Stack in FreeBSD. Gives each jail its own interfaces, routing table, and firewall rules. Kernel-native network isolation without overlay networks.
Poul-Henning Kamp: Danish FreeBSD developer. Created Jails (1999), the GEOM storage framework, and Varnish Cache. Known for phk's bikeshed argument and decades of kernel-level systems work.
Cracktro: A signature animation attached to cracked software by pirate groups in the 1980s and 1990s. Scrolling text, chiptune music, and vector effects as calling cards. The art form that evolved into the demoscene.
Dubmood: Swedish chiptune musician and demoscene legend. Two decades of tracker music across Amiga, C64, and PC platforms. Composed the soundtrack for Razor 1911's 'The Scene Is Dead' (2012).
Linuxulator: FreeBSD's Linux binary compatibility layer. Translates Linux system calls to FreeBSD equivalents in the kernel, running Linux binaries natively without emulation. Active since 1995.
ABI: Application Binary Interface. The low-level contract between a binary and the operating system: syscall numbers, calling conventions, data layout. Determines which binaries run on which kernel.
DTrace: A dynamic tracing framework for real-time analysis of running systems. Originated in Solaris, ported to FreeBSD and macOS. Traces kernel and userspace with near-zero overhead when idle.
Goodhart's Law: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. Named after economist Charles Goodhart (1975). Explains why velocity tracking corrupts estimation.
Kent Beck: Creator of Extreme Programming and co-inventor of story points with Ron Jeffries. Wrote the SUnit testing framework that became JUnit. Advocate of simplicity in software design.
Paul Vixie: Author of Vixie cron (1987), BIND (the DNS server running a third of the internet), and numerous RFCs. Internet Hall of Fame inductee (2014). The five-field crontab syntax is his.
Ken Thompson: Co-creator of Unix (1969), the B programming language, and the first cron (1975) at Bell Labs. Co-designed UTF-8 and Plan 9. Turing Award winner (1983) with Dennis Ritchie.
DataLoader: A batching and caching utility for GraphQL resolvers. Collects individual field-level data requests within a single tick and executes them as one batch query. Mitigates the N+1 problem but adds a layer REST does not need.
Apollo Client: A comprehensive GraphQL client library for JavaScript. 137 KB minified. Provides caching, state management, and query orchestration. The client-side cost of choosing GraphQL over fetch().
N+1 Problem: A data-fetching antipattern where a query for N items triggers N additional queries for related data. GraphQL resolvers are structurally prone to it; REST with SQL JOINs avoids it by default.
Introspection: A GraphQL feature that allows clients to query the schema itself, discovering types, fields, and relationships at runtime. Useful for tooling, dangerous when exposed in production without access controls.
Lee Byron: Co-creator of GraphQL at Facebook (2012) with Nick Schrock and Dan Schafer. Led the open-source release in 2015 and founded the GraphQL Foundation. Also created Immutable.js.
CDN: Content Delivery Network. A geographically distributed network of servers that cache and serve content closer to the user. Reduces latency and origin load. Relies on HTTP caching semantics (GET, Cache-Control).
seccomp: Secure Computing mode. A Linux kernel facility that restricts which system calls a process may invoke. Strict mode (2005) permits four calls. seccomp-bpf (2012) allows programmable filters via BPF programmes.
BPF: Berkeley Packet Filter. Originally a packet capture mechanism, extended in Linux as eBPF for programmable kernel-level filtering. Used by seccomp-bpf to inspect and decide on every syscall at runtime.
Ambient Authority: The security property where a process inherits all the permissions of the user who launched it. The default Unix model since 1969. Capsicum eliminates it; seccomp restricts it.
Landlock: A Linux security module (merged in 5.13, 2021) that adds unprivileged filesystem sandboxing. Moves closer to Capsicum's capability model but does not yet match the simplicity of cap_enter().
Robert Watson: Computer scientist at the University of Cambridge. Co-created Capsicum with Jonathan Anderson (2010, Best Student Paper at USENIX Security). Also contributed to FreeBSD's MAC framework and TrustedBSD.
Jonathan Anderson: Computer scientist, co-creator of Capsicum with Robert Watson. Their 2010 USENIX Security paper introduced capability-based sandboxing to FreeBSD. Now at Memorial University of Newfoundland.
Andrea Arcangeli: Linux kernel developer. Created the original seccomp strict mode in 2005 (Linux 2.6.12), permitting only four syscalls. Also known for work on KVM and Transparent Huge Pages.
Will Drewry: Google engineer who introduced seccomp-bpf in Linux 3.5 (2012). Extended seccomp from a binary four-syscall mode to programmable BPF-based filtering, enabling practical process sandboxing on Linux.
jq: A functional programming language for transforming JSON on the command line. Generators, backtracking, immutable values, compiled to a stack-based bytecode VM. 822 KB binary. Written in C by Stephen Dolan in 2012. MIT licence.
Stephen Dolan: Creator of jq (2012). PhD in Algebraic Subtyping at the University of Cambridge, supervised by Alan Mycroft. Now at Jane Street, working on the OCaml compiler. Wrote a functional language for JSON in 510 KB of C.
Michael Färber: Computer scientist. Published the first formal specification of the jq language in 2024 (arXiv:2403.20132), twelve years after Dolan wrote the implementation. Also wrote jaq, a Rust reimplementation of jq that is 5-10x faster.
Alan Mycroft: Professor of Computing at the University of Cambridge. Supervised Stephen Dolan's PhD in Algebraic Subtyping. Co-designed the Nanopass framework and contributed to static analysis research spanning four decades.
Share-Nothing: An architecture where each request handler starts with no shared state. Every request is isolated. PHP's default model. Beautiful for safety, structural impediment for persistent connections.
GIL: Global Interpreter Lock. A mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, serialising CPU-bound work. Elegant until load disagrees.
PHP-FPM: FastCGI Process Manager for PHP. Manages a pool of PHP worker processes, each handling one request at a time. Typical memory: 40-128 MB per worker.
Goroutine: A lightweight thread managed by the Go runtime. Multiplexed onto OS threads. Enables high concurrency but subject to garbage collection pauses.
RAII: Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation. A pattern where resources are tied to object lifetimes: acquired in the constructor, released in the destructor. Rust enforces this through ownership. No garbage collector needed.
Rolling Release: A software distribution model where updates ship continuously rather than in discrete versions. No fixed release date, no frozen state. The system is always current, never finished.
SemVer: Semantic Versioning. A versioning scheme using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH where major means breaking changes, minor means new features, patch means bug fixes. The contract is voluntary and routinely violated.
Dependabot: A GitHub-integrated bot that automatically opens pull requests to update dependencies. On a monorepo, it can generate hundreds of PRs per week, each requiring review and testing.
LTS: Long-Term Support. A release designated for extended maintenance, typically receiving only security patches and critical bug fixes. Node.js LTS lasts 30 months. PostgreSQL supports each major for 5 years.
Debian: A community-driven Linux distribution known for its stability-first release policy. Debian Stable freezes packages and releases when ready, not on a schedule. The reference for conservative, tested software deployment.
CrowdStrike Incident: On 19 July 2024, a rapid content update to CrowdStrike's Falcon Sensor bypassed quality control and crashed 8.5 million Windows machines globally. Airlines, hospitals, banks, and emergency services were affected. $5.4 billion in Fortune 500 damages.
Enshittification: The process by which platforms degrade their product to extract value. Coined by Cory Doctorow in 2022. Three phases: attract users, lock in businesses at users' expense, extract value for shareholders at everyone's expense. Word of the Year 2023 (American Dialect Society) and 2024 (Macquarie Dictionary).
Organic Reach: The percentage of a page's followers who see a post without paid promotion. Facebook organic reach for business pages fell from 16% in 2012 to 1.37% in 2024, the canonical example of platform enshittification.
Network Effect: The phenomenon where a product becomes more valuable as more people use it. The mechanism that creates lock-in: leaving the network means losing the connections you built on it.
Cory Doctorow: Canadian-British author, journalist, and digital rights activist. Coined 'enshittification' in November 2022. Published on Pluralistic. Won Word of the Year 2023. Author of Little Brother, Walkaway, and the 2025 book Enshittification.
diff: A Unix utility that compares two files and outputs the lines that differ. Created by James Hunt and Douglas McIlroy at Bell Labs in 1974. Every code review, pull request, and version control system is built on diff.
patch: A Unix utility that applies a diff output to a file, reproducing the changes. Written by Larry Wall in 1985. Before the web, developers exchanged patches via email and Usenet to collaborate on code.
Douglas McIlroy: Bell Labs computer scientist. Invented Unix pipes (1973), diff (1974), spell, join, tr, and echo. Head of the Computing Techniques Research Department that created Unix. His 1964 'garden hose' memo described pipes nine years before implementation.
Larry Wall: Creator of the patch utility (1985) and the Perl programming language (1987). patch enabled collaborative development over email and Usenet before the web existed.
Eugene Myers: Computer scientist who published 'An O(ND) Difference Algorithm' in 1986. His key insight: finding the shortest edit script is equivalent to finding the shortest path in a graph. Git uses Myers as its default diff algorithm.
RCS: Revision Control System. Created by Walter Tichy in 1982. The first version control system to store history as a sequence of diffs rather than full file copies. Predecessor to CVS and the delta-based model Git inherited.
Conway's Law: The observation by Melvin Conway (1967) that organisations design systems mirroring their own communication structure. Teams that do not talk produce components that do not integrate.
Melvin Conway: Computer scientist who formulated Conway's Law in 1967: organisations produce system designs that mirror their communication structures. The observation underpins every argument about team topology and software architecture.
Agile Manifesto: A 2001 document signed by 17 software developers at Snowbird, Utah, articulating four values and twelve principles for iterative, human-centred software development. The founding document of the Agile movement.
SAFe: Scaled Agile Framework. A framework for applying Agile practices across large organisations, sold with certification programmes. Critiqued as commercially captured process-weight that contradicts the Agile Manifesto's values.
Story Points: Relative size units used in Agile estimation to compare work items without committing to time estimates. Invented during Extreme Programming as a deliberate abstraction; widely misapplied as a productivity metric.
Velocity: In Scrum, the sum of story points completed per sprint. Originally a private team planning tool; widely misapplied as a cross-team productivity KPI.
XP: Extreme Programming. Agile software development methodology created by Kent Beck in 1996. The source from which Scrum borrowed its ceremonies while leaving behind XP's engineering practices: TDD, pair programming, continuous integration.
Kanban: Pull-based workflow management method originating in Toyota's manufacturing system. Work items flow through stages only when capacity exists downstream. No sprints, no estimates, no velocity metrics.
Frederick Winslow Taylor: American mechanical engineer who published 'The Principles of Scientific Management' (1911), introducing time-and-motion studies. The article on Agile argues corporate Scrum is Taylorism with different vocabulary.
Trygve Reenskaug: Norwegian computer scientist who invented the Model-View-Controller pattern at Xerox PARC in 1979 while working on Smalltalk desktop applications. MVC was designed for stateful GUIs, not stateless HTTP.
Edgar F. Codd: British computer scientist at IBM who published the relational model of data in 1970. His paper 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks' is the theoretical foundation of SQL databases.
Kyle Kingsbury: Software engineer known as Aphyr. Author of the Jepsen distributed systems testing framework. His analyses demonstrated data loss and safety violations in MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra, and other databases.
Jepsen: Open-source framework by Kyle Kingsbury for testing distributed system correctness under network partitions, clock skew, and crashes. The gold standard for verifying database safety guarantees.
Daniel Stenberg: Swedish software engineer, creator and sole maintainer of curl since 1998. curl now ships with virtually every operating system and runs on billions of devices.
Donald Knuth: Computer scientist, creator of TeX, and author of 'The Art of Computer Programming'. Known for the observation that premature optimisation is the root of all evil, and for numbering TeX versions converging to pi.
Erich Gamma: Swiss software engineer, co-author of 'Design Patterns' (1994) and co-creator of JUnit with Kent Beck. Also created the Eclipse IDE and Visual Studio Code's language server architecture.
Tobias Koppers: German software engineer, creator of Webpack (2012). Now working on Turbopack at Vercel.
Evan Wallace: Co-founder of Figma and creator of esbuild. Wrote esbuild in Go, exploiting parallelism and avoiding AST serialisation to achieve bundling speeds 10-100x faster than Webpack.
Bjorn Ottosson: Swedish graphics programmer who published the Oklab perceptual colour space in 2020, designed from first principles to match human vision uniformly across all hues. The mathematical model underlying OKLCH.
Matt Holt: Author of Caddy, the web server that decided the TLS configuration problem should not exist. Caddy was the first major server to automate HTTPS by default.
Nicole Sullivan: Front-end developer who published Object-Oriented CSS (OOCSS) in 2009 at Yahoo. Introduced the principles of separating structure from skin and container from content.
Nick Schrock: Software engineer at Facebook who co-created GraphQL in 2012 alongside Dan Schafer. GraphQL was open-sourced in 2015.
Dan Schafer: Software engineer at Facebook who co-created GraphQL alongside Nick Schrock. Now at Apollo GraphQL.
Natalie Weizenbaum: Software engineer who co-created Sass and was its primary maintainer. Led the development of Dart Sass, the current reference implementation.
Daniel Hartmeier: OpenBSD developer who wrote pf (Packet Filter) in 2001 as a replacement for IPFilter. His design incorporated rate limiting and overload tables as first-class features.
Solomon Hykes: Founder of Docker, who demonstrated the technology at PyCon 2013 in a five-minute lightning talk titled 'The Future of Linux Containers'. Departed Docker in 2018.
Kelsey Hightower: Former Google Staff Developer Advocate. Author of 'Kubernetes the Hard Way'. Famously stated: 'Monoliths are the future' and 'You haven't mastered a tool until you understand when it should not be used.'
Simon Wardley: British business strategist known for Wardley Maps and value-chain analysis. Called LLMs 'coherence engines, not truth engines'.
David Kriesel: German data scientist who discovered and publicised the Xerox WorkCentre JBIG2 number-substitution bug in 2013, where a scanner silently replaced digits in scanned documents.
BEM: Block Element Modifier. A CSS naming convention from Yandex (2005) using the syntax .block__element--modifier. Prevents class name collisions in large shared stylesheets. Largely obsoleted by CSS Modules, Shadow DOM, @scope, and CSS nesting.
Virtual DOM: In-memory representation of the real DOM used by React and similar frameworks to batch updates and compute minimal diff patches. Introduces approximately 30% overhead compared to direct DOM manipulation.
Hydration: The process by which a JavaScript framework re-attaches event listeners and state to server-rendered HTML in the browser. Blocks interactivity until complete.
JSX: JavaScript syntax extension standardised by Facebook. Allows HTML-like markup in JavaScript files. Must be transpiled to function calls before execution.
Cold Start: Latency penalty incurred when a serverless function must provision a new container instance, as no warm instance is available. Typically 100 ms to several seconds depending on runtime.
Tree-shaking: Dead code elimination performed by bundlers at build time, removing unused exports from the final output by statically analysing the import graph. Only effective with ES module static syntax.
Service Mesh: An infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication in microservice architectures. Provides mutual TLS, observability, and traffic management via sidecar proxies.
Istio: The most widely deployed service mesh for Kubernetes. Adds traffic management, observability, and security via sidecar proxies. Overhead per pod: 2.5 ms latency, 0.20 vCPU, 60 MB memory.
mTLS: Mutual TLS. Extension of TLS where both client and server authenticate each other with certificates. Used by service meshes to encrypt and authenticate inter-service traffic.
etcd: Distributed key-value store used by Kubernetes to persist all cluster state. Every API operation reads from or writes to etcd, making it a performance and availability bottleneck.
Shadow DOM: A browser API that encapsulates a component's DOM subtree and stylesheet from the main document, providing native style isolation without naming conventions.
Web Components: Browser-native standard for creating reusable custom HTML elements with encapsulated Shadow DOM and lifecycle callbacks, requiring no framework or build step.
htmx: JavaScript library (14 KB) that extends HTML with hx- attributes for making AJAX requests and updating DOM fragments, enabling dynamic behaviour without writing JavaScript or building a SPA.
Container Queries: CSS feature allowing an element to apply styles based on the dimensions of its containing element rather than the viewport. Solves component-level responsive design.
Eventual Consistency: Distributed systems property where replicas may be temporarily out of sync but will converge to the same state given sufficient time and no further updates.
Supply Chain Attack: A security attack targeting a dependency in the software delivery pipeline rather than the application itself. Compromising a widely-used npm package can affect millions of downstream projects.
Vendor Lock-in: Condition where dependence on a vendor's proprietary formats, APIs, or workflows makes switching to a competitor impractical without significant cost or data loss.
BSL: Business Source License. Time-limited source-available licence adopted by HashiCorp for Terraform in 2023. Source is viewable but commercial use is restricted until a specified date, then converts to open-source.
SSPL: Server Side Public License. Created by MongoDB Inc. in 2018, requiring that any software offering MongoDB as a service must open-source the entire service stack. Not recognised as open-source by the OSI.
OpenTofu: Open-source fork of Terraform maintained by the Linux Foundation, created after HashiCorp relicensed Terraform under the BSL in August 2023. Drop-in compatible with existing configurations.
Valkey: Open-source fork of Redis maintained by the Linux Foundation, created after Redis Ltd. relicensed Redis under the SSPL in March 2024. Supported by AWS, Google Cloud, and Oracle.
GPL: GNU General Public License. Copyleft licence requiring that derivative works also be distributed under the GPL. Linux is licensed under GPLv2.
BSD Licence: Permissive open-source licence from the University of California, Berkeley. Allows use, modification, and distribution with minimal restrictions. FreeBSD uses a 2-clause BSD licence.
TDD: Test-Driven Development. Practice of writing a failing test before writing the code that satisfies it, then refactoring. Popularised by Kent Beck alongside Extreme Programming.
Borrow Checker: Rust's compile-time mechanism that enforces ownership and borrowing rules, guaranteeing memory safety without a garbage collector. Prevents use-after-free, data races, and null pointer dereferences.
CommonJS: JavaScript module format used in Node.js, using require() to import and module.exports to export. Does not support static analysis, preventing effective tree-shaking. Being superseded by ES Modules.
AST: Abstract Syntax Tree. Tree-structured representation of source code produced by a parser. Bundlers and transpilers traverse and transform the AST; serialisation overhead is the primary source of Webpack's speed deficit.
ARIA: Accessible Rich Internet Applications. A W3C specification defining roles, states, and properties that add accessibility semantics to HTML elements beyond their native meaning.
Firecracker: Open-source MicroVM technology developed by AWS, used to run Lambda functions and Fargate tasks. Boots a minimal VM in under 125 milliseconds while providing hardware-level isolation.
Backpressure: Flow control mechanism where a downstream consumer signals an upstream producer to slow output when the consumer cannot keep pace. Prevents memory exhaustion in streaming pipelines.
OpenZFS: Open-source ZFS implementation maintaining a unified codebase for FreeBSD and Linux since 2013. Provides feature parity across platforms through a single upstream project.
Btrfs: Copy-on-write filesystem for Linux providing snapshots and subvolumes. The primary Linux alternative to ZFS. Basic operations are production-grade; RAID 5/6 support remains unreliable.
Boot Environment: Complete, bootable ZFS snapshot of an operating system installation. Allows rolling back to a previous system state by selecting a different boot environment at boot time.
SRE: Site Reliability Engineering. Discipline pioneered at Google that applies software engineering practices to operations. SREs define and monitor Service Level Objectives and manage error budgets.
FinOps: Financial Operations. Discipline combining finance and engineering to manage and optimise cloud spend, bringing financial accountability to variable-cost cloud infrastructure.
chroot: A Unix system call that changes the apparent root directory for a process, providing the earliest form of filesystem isolation (1979). The conceptual ancestor of containers and jails.
LXC: Linux Containers. A containerisation runtime using namespaces and cgroups, predating Docker by five years. Used as Docker's original backend before libcontainer.
Alpine Linux: A minimal Linux distribution based on musl libc and BusyBox. Widely used as a Docker base image to reduce image size. Its musl libc can break native Node.js modules that assume glibc.
Oklab: A perceptual colour space published by Bjorn Ottosson in 2020, designed to match human vision uniformly across all hues. The Cartesian form from which OKLCH is derived.
sRGB: Standard RGB. The colour space defined in 1996 for CRT monitors, used as the baseline for HEX and RGB values in CSS. Modern wide-gamut displays exceed its range.
Display P3: A wide-gamut colour space used by modern Apple and Samsung displays, offering roughly 50% more colours than sRGB. Addressable in CSS via OKLCH but not via HEX or RGB.
HSL: Hue, Saturation, Lightness. A CSS colour model expressing colours in human-intuitive terms but with a mathematical lightness value that does not match perceived brightness across hues.
p99 Latency: The 99th-percentile response time, indicating the worst latency experienced by 1% of requests. The standard metric for tail latency in distributed systems.
CSS Grid: CSS layout module for two-dimensional layout, controlling both rows and columns simultaneously. Supersedes many use cases that previously required float hacks or Flexbox workarounds.
Flexbox: CSS layout module for one-dimensional alignment along a single axis (row or column). Universally supported since 2015. Often misused for two-dimensional layouts where CSS Grid is the correct tool.
Procedural Generation: Creating content (geometry, textures, music) algorithmically at runtime from mathematical rules rather than storing pre-authored assets. The technique that makes 64 KB demoscene productions possible.
Raymarching: A rendering technique that traces rays through a scene by iteratively stepping along a signed distance function. Enables full 3D rendering without triangle meshes. Widely used in demoscene intros.
SDF: Signed Distance Function. A mathematical function returning the distance from any point to the nearest surface. Used in demoscene intros to define complex geometry in bytes rather than megabytes of mesh data.
Razor 1911: Norwegian demoscene group founded in 1985, one of the oldest surviving groups. Originated as a cracking group on the Amiga, later transitioned to demo production spanning four decades.
Conspiracy: Hungarian demoscene group responsible for Clean Slate (2021) and Chaos Theory (2006). Known for technically extreme 64K productions using their in-house apEx demo engine.
Prismbeings: Demoscene group that created Zetsubo (2018, Revision), a 4 KB intro featuring raymarched procedural geometry and a software-synthesised atmospheric soundtrack.
JSDoc: JavaScript documentation standard using comment blocks with @param and @returns tags. With @type annotations, provides full IDE type support via the TypeScript language server without a build step.
esbuild: JavaScript and TypeScript bundler written in Go. 10-100x faster than Webpack by avoiding AST serialisation overhead. Created by Evan Wallace.
OpenSSH: The open-source implementation of the SSH protocol suite, maintained by the OpenBSD project. First released in 1999 by Theo de Raadt. Ships with virtually every Unix-like operating system.
Jenkins: Open-source automation server widely used for CI/CD, configured via Groovy pipelines. The archetypal legacy CI system, largely superseded by GitHub Actions and GitLab CI.
GitHub Actions: GitHub's integrated CI/CD platform using YAML workflow files and a marketplace of reusable actions. Dominant in open-source projects since 2019.
JBIG2: A compression standard for bi-level images using pattern matching. Central to the Xerox scanner bug where a scanner silently substituted digits in scanned documents by matching similar-looking number shapes.
Normalisation: Organising a relational database schema to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity by applying normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF). The discipline MongoDB told you to skip.
:has(): CSS relational pseudo-class (Baseline 2023) that selects an element based on what it contains, enabling parent selection for the first time in CSS history. Replaces JavaScript for state-dependent parent styling.
:is(): CSS pseudo-class that groups multiple selectors into one, adopting the specificity of its most specific argument. Eliminates selector repetition.
:where(): CSS pseudo-class with identical grouping behaviour to :is() but with always-zero specificity. Ideal for defaults and reset layers that should never win a specificity contest.
RFC 6455: The IETF standard defining the WebSocket protocol (2011). Specifies the HTTP upgrade handshake, frame format (2-byte minimum header), ping/pong heartbeats, and close semantics for persistent full-duplex communication.
Socket.IO: A JavaScript library for real-time communication between browser and server. 46 KB client bundle plus transitive npm dependencies. Provides fallbacks and reconnection logic that the native WebSocket API does not need.
Long Polling: A technique where the client sends an HTTP request and the server holds it open until data is available, then responds and the client immediately reconnects. A 2006 workaround for the lack of persistent connections, superseded by WebSocket.
Full-Duplex: A communication mode where both endpoints can send and receive data simultaneously over the same channel. WebSocket is full-duplex; HTTP request-response is half-duplex.
axum: A Rust web framework built on top of tokio and hyper. Provides ergonomic routing, middleware, and WebSocket support with two crate dependencies. Maintained by the tokio team.
gorilla/websocket: A Go package implementing the WebSocket protocol. Archived but stable. The de facto standard for WebSocket servers in Go before the standard library gained its own implementation.
0b5vr: Japanese demoscener and creative coder. Created 0mix (Revision 2023), a 64 KB browser-based techno live set that won Best Soundtrack at The Meteoriks 2024. Builds custom WebGL frameworks. Source code published on GitHub.
The Meteoriks: The demoscene's annual awards ceremony, recognising outstanding productions across categories including Best Demo, Best Intro, Best Soundtrack, and Outstanding Concept. The demoscene equivalent of the Academy Awards.
Revision: The world's largest demoparty, held annually in Saarbruecken, Germany. Draws over 1,000 sceners from across Europe for competitions in demos, intros, graphics, music, and wild categories.
Algorave: A movement combining live coding and electronic dance music. Performers write synthesis and sequencing code on stage, projected for the audience. The code is the instrument.
GLSL: OpenGL Shading Language. A C-like language for programming GPU shaders. Used in demoscene productions to generate visuals and audio from mathematical functions in real time.
WebGL: A JavaScript API for rendering 2D and 3D graphics in the browser using the GPU. Based on OpenGL ES. Runs without plugins in every modern browser.
FM Synthesis: Frequency Modulation synthesis. A sound generation technique where one oscillator modulates the frequency of another, producing complex timbres from simple waveforms. Used in the Yamaha DX7 and in demoscene audio engines.
Robert C. Martin: Software engineer and author. Published Clean Code (2008) and the SOLID principles. Influential in the Agile movement. His prescriptions for Java enterprise codebases were widely adopted, often without the original context.
Clean Code: A 2008 book by Robert C. Martin prescribing small functions, minimal arguments, and relentless extraction. Written for Java enterprise monoliths with no structure. Widely adopted as universal law regardless of language or context.
FitNesse: An open-source acceptance testing framework co-developed by Robert C. Martin. The chapter 3 refactoring example in Clean Code uses a FitNesse module to demonstrate function extraction.
Dan Abramov: Software engineer, co-creator of Redux and member of the React core team at Meta. Wrote 'Goodbye, Clean Code' (2020), reflecting on the dangers of over-zealous refactoring and premature abstraction.
Lean Code: Vivian Voss's coding methodology: one function, one job, readable at first glance. Prioritises clarity through simplicity over line-count rules. KISS in your head, not rules on a wall.
Jira: A project tracking tool by Atlassian, originally a bug tracker (2002). Grew into a workflow engine, compliance surface, and reporting layer. 68% of new users report feeling overwhelmed in their first month.
Atlassian: Australian software company founded in 2002. Makes Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, and Trello. Revenue $5.2 billion (FY2025). Killed Jira Server in February 2024, forcing Cloud or Data Center migration.
JQL: Jira Query Language. A proprietary query syntax that resembles SQL closely enough to mislead, then diverges: no joins, no subqueries, useful in one product.
Linear: A project tracking tool built by engineers who left Uber. $1.25 billion valuation, 100 employees, $35,000 total marketing spend. Loads in under one second. The product is the marketing.
Karri Saarinen: Co-founder and CEO of Linear. Previously design lead at Airbnb and Coinbase. Left Uber partly due to frustration with Jira's complexity and built Linear as the alternative.
JetBrains Space: A collaborative development platform by JetBrains integrating project management, code hosting, and CI/CD with IDE support. Launched 2020, discontinued 2024. A better product that lost to inertia.
Early Access: A distribution model where unfinished software or games are sold to fund development. Players pay a reduced price, receive an incomplete product, and provide feedback. Formalised on Steam in March 2013 with twelve titles.
Markus Persson: Swedish game developer, known as Notch. Created Minecraft in 2009, sold the alpha for ten euros with no publisher. Four million copies before release. Sold Mojang to Microsoft for $2.5 billion in 2014.
Minecraft: A sandbox video game created by Markus Persson in 2009. Alpha sold for ten euros, released November 2011. Over 300 million copies sold, making it the best-selling video game in history. The original early access success story.
Supergiant Games: American indie game studio. Developed Bastion, Transistor, Pyre, and Hades. Sold 700,000 copies of Hades during early access, redesigned the narrative around player feedback, and shipped a Game of the Year.
Hades: A roguelike action game by Supergiant Games. Entered early access in December 2018, sold 700,000 copies during EA, and won Game of the Year 2020. Its sequel Hades II sold two million copies in early access.
Larian Studios: Belgian game developer founded in 1996. Spent three years developing Baldur's Gate 3 in early access, sold 2.5 million copies before launch, and shipped a game that won six Game of the Year awards.
Baldur's Gate 3: A role-playing game by Larian Studios. Entered early access in October 2020, sold 2.5 million copies during EA, fully released August 2023. Six GOTY awards. Swen Vincke said it would never get made without early access.
Swen Vincke: Belgian game designer, founder and CEO of Larian Studios. Led the development of the Divinity series and Baldur's Gate 3. Publicly stated that the game would never have been made without early access funding and feedback.
Kerbal Space Program: An orbital mechanics simulator by Squad, a Mexican indie studio. Entered Steam Early Access in March 2013, released April 2015. Endorsed by NASA for its physics accuracy.
CD Projekt Red: Polish game developer. Created The Witcher series and Cyberpunk 2077. Shipped Cyberpunk in December 2020 with critical bugs, causing Sony to delist it and $1.8 billion in market value loss.
Cyberpunk 2077: An open-world RPG by CD Projekt Red. Shipped December 2020 with severe bugs. 13 million copies in ten days. Sony delisted it. Full refunds offered. $1.8 billion in market cap evaporated. A full-price early access title that never called itself one.
Fntastic: Game studio behind The Day Before (2023). Promised an open-world MMO, delivered a broken extraction shooter. 18.5% positive reviews. Studio closed four days after launch. 91,000 of 201,000 copies refunded.
Steam Early Access: A programme launched by Valve on 20 March 2013 with twelve titles. Allows developers to sell unfinished games on Steam. Only 25% of Early Access titles reach full release.
Markdown: A lightweight markup language created by John Gruber in 2004. Designed to be readable as plain text and convertible to HTML. 84% admiration score in Stack Overflow 2024 survey.
CommonMark: A formal specification for Markdown syntax, begun in 2014 by John MacFarlane and Jeff Atwood. Provides 500+ conformance tests to eliminate ambiguity in the original Markdown spec.
John Gruber: Creator of Markdown (2004). Published the original specification as a 1,122-line Perl script with the design goal that a Markdown document should be publishable as-is, as plain text.
Mermaid: A JavaScript-based diagramming tool that renders flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ER diagrams, and Gantt charts from Markdown-like text syntax. Supported natively by GitHub, GitLab, and most Markdown editors.
Confluence: Atlassian's team wiki and documentation platform. Stores content in proprietary XML format. Exports lose macros, user mappings, and formatting.
Typora: A Markdown editor with live rendering, Mermaid support, LaTeX, and export to PDF, HTML, and ePub. One-time purchase, not subscription.
ZFSBootMenu: A Linux boot manager that replaces GRUB entirely, providing native ZFS boot environment support. Third-party, unlike FreeBSD's built-in bectl.
zsys: Ubuntu's abandoned ZFS management daemon. Provided automatic snapshots and boot environments. Development ceased in 2021.
bectl: FreeBSD boot environment management tool, in the base system since FreeBSD 12.0 (2018). Creates, lists, and activates ZFS boot environments without third-party packages.
snapper: A tool for managing Btrfs and LVM snapshots on Linux. Used by openSUSE for automatic pre-upgrade snapshots with GRUB rollback support.
Subgrid: CSS Grid feature allowing a child grid to adopt the track sizing of its parent grid. Enables aligned layouts across nested components without JavaScript. Baseline 2023, 93% browser support.
scroll-snap: A CSS feature (scroll-snap-type, scroll-snap-align) that provides native carousel and slider behaviour without JavaScript. The browser handles snapping between items.
man pages: Unix manual pages, the standard documentation format since 1971. Nine sections covering commands, system calls, library functions, file formats, and system administration. Accessible offline via the man command.
Midnight Commander: A dual-panel text-mode file manager for Unix. Written in C by Miguel de Icaza in 1994. 220,000 lines, 12 MB RAM, built-in editor, viewer, diff, FTP, SFTP, and archive browsing.
Norton Commander: A dual-panel file manager for DOS, written by John Socha in 1984. Became so ubiquitous in the Soviet Union that 'Norton' became the Russian word for file manager. Spawned mc, Total Commander, and FAR Manager.
Miguel de Icaza: Mexican software developer. Created Midnight Commander (1994), co-founded GNOME, created Mono (.NET for Linux), and Xamarin (acquired by Microsoft in 2016).
John Socha: Software developer who wrote Norton Commander in 1984 as a PhD student at Cornell. First director of R&D at Peter Norton Computing.
Eugene Roshal: Russian software developer. Created RAR (1993) and FAR Manager (1996). One of the most quietly productive programmers in computing history.
Christian Ghisler: Swiss software developer. Created Total Commander (1993, originally Windows Commander). One man, thirty-three years of shareware development.
ncurses: A library for writing text-based user interfaces in a terminal. Provides cursor movement, window management, colour, and keyboard input. The foundation of TUI applications like mc, htop, and tmux.
FISH: Files transferred over SHell protocol. Used by Midnight Commander to browse remote filesystems over SSH without requiring SCP or SFTP on the target machine. Needs only a basic shell.
Filippo Valsorda: Italian-American cryptographer. Created age (2019), a modern file encryption tool. Previously maintained Go's cryptography libraries at Google.
scp: Secure Copy Protocol. Copies files between hosts over SSH. Part of the OpenSSH suite. Being deprecated in favour of sftp due to protocol limitations.
age: A modern file encryption tool by Filippo Valsorda (2019). Single binary, no configuration, no key servers. Designed as a simpler replacement for GPG's encryption use case.
LLM: Large Language Model. A neural network trained on text data to generate, complete, and reason about natural language. GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama are LLMs.
Smalltalk: An object-oriented programming language created at Xerox PARC in the 1970s. Pioneered GUI concepts, the IDE, and the MVC pattern. Everything is an object, including numbers and classes.
Xerox PARC: Palo Alto Research Center. Xerox's research lab that invented the graphical user interface, Ethernet, laser printing, Smalltalk, and the MVC pattern. Most inventions were commercialised by others.
Ruby on Rails: A web framework created by David Heinemeier Hansson in 2004. Popularised MVC for web development and convention over configuration. Built on Ruby.
content-visibility: A CSS property that defers rendering of off-screen content until the user scrolls to it. Significant performance improvement for long pages, with zero JavaScript.
showModal(): A method on the native HTML dialog element that opens it as a modal with automatic backdrop, focus trapping, and ESC-to-close behaviour. One line of JavaScript replaces entire modal libraries.
prefers-reduced-motion: A CSS media query that detects whether the user has requested minimal animation in their operating system preferences. A basic accessibility courtesy, one media query.
color-scheme: An HTML meta tag and CSS property that tells the browser the page supports light and dark colour schemes. The browser adapts form controls and scrollbars automatically, without JavaScript.
minline.js: A DOM-based templating engine by Vivian Voss, compressed to exactly 1,337 bytes. Part of the Min2Max ecosystem. Includes a dark/light theme wizard.
David Heinemeier Hansson: Danish programmer, creator of Ruby on Rails (2004). Known as DHH. Popularised MVC architecture for web development and the philosophy of convention over configuration.
htop: An interactive process viewer for Unix systems, created by Hisham Muhammad in 2004. Replaced the workflow of top, ps, grep, kill, and renice with a single interactive binary. 12,000 lines of C.
Hisham Muhammad: Brazilian software developer. Created htop (2004), LuaRocks (the Lua package manager), and co-founded GoboLinux. Handed htop to the community in 2020 after sixteen years of solo maintenance.
GoboLinux: A Linux distribution co-founded by Hisham Muhammad that uses an alternative filesystem hierarchy where each program lives in its own directory tree, eliminating the need for a package database.
QNX: A commercial real-time operating system with a microkernel architecture. In 1999, QNX fit a complete POSIX OS with GUI, web browser, and TCP/IP stack on a single 1.44 MB floppy disk. Now runs in 215 million vehicles.
BeOS: A media-focused operating system by Be Inc. Released as a free Personal Edition in 2000, downloaded one million times. Featured pervasive multithreading and boot times in seconds. Acquired by Palm for $11 million, source code lost.
NeXTSTEP: An object-oriented operating system by NeXT (Steve Jobs). Where Tim Berners-Lee wrote the first web browser and id Software built Doom. Interface Builder in 1988. Became the foundation of macOS.
Jean-Louis Gassee: French businessman and engineer. Founded Be Inc. and created BeOS. Previously Apple's president of product development. Steve Jobs wanted Apple to acquire BeOS, but Apple chose NeXT instead.
TIC-80: A fantasy console that deliberately mimics 1980s hardware constraints: 240 by 136 pixel display, 16 colour palette, 256 sprites, 4 audio channels. Programs written in Lua or JavaScript.
Spectrox: A demoscene group. Their production Prelude to Neobyte (2025) placed second at Deadline Berlin, running on the TIC-80 fantasy console with voxel landscapes, copper bar effects, and an underwater endscroller.
Virgill: Jochen Feldkoetter, a demoscene musician composing since the early 1990s. Member of Alcatraz and Haujobb. Created AmigaKlang, a modular synthesiser for Amiga productions.
AmigaKlang: A modular synthesiser for Amiga demoscene productions, created by Virgill (Jochen Feldkoetter). Designed for creating music within the extreme constraints of Amiga hardware.
Olympian: A demoscene coder and graphics artist. Created the code and visuals for Spectrox's Prelude to Neobyte (2025), a TIC-80 fantasy console demo.
Next.js: A React meta-framework by Vercel. Adds server-side rendering, routing, and build tooling on top of React. Ships 87 KB of client runtime (App Router) before application code. Fresh project: 247 MB of node_modules.
Vercel: A cloud platform for frontend deployment, founded by Guillermo Rauch in 2015. Maintains Next.js. The framework's features (next/image, ISR, Middleware) assume Vercel's infrastructure.
hydration: The process where a client-side JavaScript framework downloads its runtime, parses it, and re-renders a server-sent HTML page to attach event handlers and make it interactive. Two rendering passes for one page.
Turbopack: A JavaScript bundler written in Rust by Vercel. Announced October 2022, stable 2025. Replaces Webpack as the default bundler for Next.js development. Created because JavaScript was too slow to bundle JavaScript.
ISR: Incremental Static Regeneration. A Next.js feature that regenerates static pages on demand after deployment. Persists to Vercel's edge storage by default, complicating self-hosting.
OpenNext: An open-source adapter project created by Cloudflare and Netlify to make Next.js deployable outside Vercel. The framework's maintainer would not provide this portability.
Guillermo Rauch: Argentine software developer. CEO of Vercel. Created Socket.IO and Mongoose. Founded Vercel (originally ZEIT) in 2015. Maintains Next.js.
Dennis Ritchie: Co-creator of Unix and inventor of the C programming language at Bell Labs. Co-authored The C Programming Language with Kernighan (1978). Turing Award 1983 with Ken Thompson. National Medal of Technology 1999. Died 12 October 2011.
Brian Kernighan: Bell Labs computer scientist. Named Unix. Co-authored The C Programming Language (K&R C) with Ritchie, and co-created AWK. Professor at Princeton University.
Bill Joy: Co-founder of Sun Microsystems. As a graduate student at Berkeley, wrote vi, the C shell, and contributed TCP/IP networking to BSD Unix. One of the most productive programmers in computing history.
Multics: Multiplexed Information and Computing Service. A time-sharing operating system begun in 1964 by MIT, General Electric, and Bell Labs. Behind schedule and over budget, it drove Bell Labs to withdraw in 1969, directly leading to Unix.
PDP-7: A minicomputer by Digital Equipment Corporation (1964). 18-bit word size, 9 KB of user memory, $72,000 list price. The machine on which Ken Thompson built the first version of Unix in August 1969.
PDP-11: A 16-bit minicomputer by Digital Equipment Corporation (1970). The machine to which Unix was first ported, and on which it was rewritten in C in 1973. One of the most influential computer architectures.
C: A systems programming language created by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973. Designed to make Unix portable. Evolved from BCPL and B. The second most used programming language in the world, fifty-three years after its creation.
B: A programming language created by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs in 1969. Derived from BCPL. Predecessor to C. Used for early Unix development on the PDP-7.
BCPL: Basic Combined Programming Language, designed by Martin Richards at Cambridge in 1967. Ancestor of B and C. Designed for compiler writing, it established the tradition of small, systems-oriented languages.
Bell Labs: Bell Telephone Laboratories. The research division of AT&T where Unix, C, the transistor, the laser, information theory, and the cosmic microwave background were discovered or invented. Home to Thompson, Ritchie, McIlroy, and Kernighan.
Space Travel: A solar system simulation game written by Ken Thompson on the GE-645 mainframe at Bell Labs. The desire to run it on cheaper hardware led Thompson to build Unix on a PDP-7 in 1969.
consent decree: A 1956 antitrust agreement that prohibited AT&T from selling products outside the telephone business. This forced AT&T to license Unix to universities for nominal fees, effectively seeding it across every computer science department.
USL: Unix System Laboratories. A subsidiary of AT&T that sued BSDi and the University of California in 1992, claiming BSD contained proprietary Unix code. The lawsuit settled in 1994: three of 18,000 files were found problematic. The two years of legal uncertainty gave Linux the window to grow.
386BSD: A free operating system by Bill Jolitz, porting BSD to the Intel 386 architecture. Released in 1992. Maintenance gaps led to the creation of FreeBSD, NetBSD, and OpenBSD as community-driven successors.
Jordan Hubbard: Co-founder of the FreeBSD project in 1993, alongside Nate Williams and Rod Grimes. Managed FreeBSD releases for over a decade. Later Director of Engineering at Apple, working on macOS and Darwin.
David Greenman: Proposed the name FreeBSD on the 386BSD mailing list on 19 June 1993. Co-founder and early contributor to the FreeBSD project.
Darwin: The open-source Unix foundation of macOS, iOS, iPadOS, watchOS, and tvOS. A BSD derivative combining the Mach microkernel with FreeBSD userland. Every Apple device runs Darwin at its core.
